text: Hi, my name is Dr. Vishal Soni and we would be watching a case of robotic cholecystectomy done for a case of symptomatic gallstone disease. First thing that you observe is an absolute stable visual platform. It helps in identification of all the anatomical structures in very precise way.

The robotic camera provides a stable view of the stomach and duodenum. Indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence highlights the extrahepatic biliary tree in green, allowing identification of the common bile duct, cystic duct, and the neck region of the gallbladder.
The surgeon divides the anterior leaf of the peritoneum overlying the cystic artery, isolating the artery through circumferential dissection in preparation for clipping.

The surgeon continues to isolate and define the cystic artery, moving towards the gallus triangle to clear fibro fatty tissue. The cystic duct is visible on the lower aspect, while the neck region of the gallbladder is on the upper aspect. The dissection proceeds in different planes due to the pear-shaped anatomy of the gallbladder, focusing now on the posterior aspect.
The surgeon adjusts the robotic camera from a thirty-degree downward angle to a thirty-degree upward angle, providing a different visual perspective to enhance dissection visibility.

The surgeon uses anatomical landmarks, such as the Rullia sulcus, to ensure the dissection remains in safe zones above this sulcus. The cystic duct is isolated circumferentially in preparation for clipping.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator to retract the gallbladder, repurposing the tool for this action. The anterior leaf of the peritoneum is divided, and anatomical landmarks are reconfirmed until reaching the Y-shaped junction of the cystic duct and common bile duct.

The surgeon identifies the vertical limb formed by the common hepatic duct and the common bile duct, and the horizontal limb formed by the cystic duct. Further dissection is performed at the Calot's triangle, with ICG confirming the absence of aberrant structures, allowing safe progression to clipping.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator to place two clips on the patient's side and one clip on the specimen side of the cystic duct and cystic artery. The clip applicator is then repurposed to retract the liver and gallbladder.

The surgeon uses a hook to divide the cystic duct with a cutting current, followed by the division of the cystic artery. The cutting current eliminates the need for scissors, and small vessels are encountered and either preserved or sacrificed based on their anatomy.

The surgeon addresses a thin, flimsy layer connecting the liver and gallbladder. Due to the gallbladder's shape, the robotic instruments are adjusted frequently to facilitate dissection, showcasing the advantage of the robotic platform's angulating capabilities.

The surgeon carefully continues the division of the flimsy tissue connecting the liver and gallbladder, ensuring precision to avoid entering the liver parenchyma or perforating the gallbladder, which could lead to bleeding or gallbladder damage.
The surgeon uses advanced robotic instruments with superior vision to precisely divide the remaining peritoneum and fibro-fatty tissue, successfully freeing the gallbladder from the liver.
The surgeon completes the surgery by checking for any bleeding or bile leaks, confirming that none are present.
Thank you for watching. Do share and subscribe and like the video.
